result:
There is a white plastic chair in the courtyard that no one claims. It arrived one ordinary Tuesday, the way sparrows arrive—without introduction, without luggage, without sound. By twilight it had become the axis on which the neighborhood spun. Children straddled it backwards, pretending it was a motorcycle that could outrun bedtime. Mrs. Kaplan rested her groceries on it while fumbling for keys. The mail carrier used it as a sorting office, fanning envelopes across the seat like a tarot spread. No one asked who brought it; asking would make it real, and real things can be taken away.  

I sit in it now at 3:07 a.m., halfway through my thousand words, watching condensation form on a glass of tap water I don’t remember pouring. The moon is a cataract over the rooftop, and the chair and I are engaged in a quiet conversation about gravity: how it keeps the water inside the glass but fails to keep mothers from drifting out of windows, how it fastens planets to orbits yet allows memories to wobble off course. The chair says nothing, but its hollow legs hold the night air like lungs, exhaling a small plastic creak each time I shift. I think this is how planets speak to one another—slow, groaning syllables across centuries, dialects of pressure and release.

Yesterday I opened a drawer and found a pamphlet from 1998 titled “So Your Spouse Has Time-Traveled Without You.” The cover shows a cartoon man waving goodbye to a wife who is dissolving into clock gears. I laughed until I tasted iron, then folded the pamphlet into a paper boat and floated it in the bathroom sink. It hovered for a moment, ink bleeding into water like squid escaping a predator, then sank, taking with it the only proof that grief can be bureaucratic, laminated, tri-folded. I wanted to call someone about the discovery, but the only number I still know by heart is my childhood landline, discontinued in 2003, now probably routing telemarketers to dead air or to some stranger who has never heard my mother’s voice ask, “Dinner’s warm, where are you?”

Memory works like that: you dial what you remember, and strangers answer.

Across the alley, a window flickers blue with television. Someone is always awake, confirming the universe hasn’t run out of light. I imagine a woman in bunny slippers applauding a late-night chef who never burns the risotto. I imagine she applauds because she once scorched dinner for her father the night before he died, and now she claps for every unscorched thing, an absolution in studio-audience form. The sound of her clapping is too soft to cross the courtyard, but I borrow it anyway, press it against my chest like a sticker that says I WAS BRAVE TODAY. The chair accepts the applause on my behalf, absorbs it into its polypropylene veins, and for a moment glows faintly, radioactive with shared mercy.

Words left: 637. I could spend them describing the smell of cut grass at dawn, metallic and baptismal, but someone else already did that in a poem people share on funeral programs. I could describe the mole on my lover’s back, how it looks like the dot above a lowercase i, proofreading her spine, but lovers tend to become geography that no longer exists—tectonic kisses, subduction zone promises. Instead I’ll mention the ant hauling a sunflower seed across the chair’s seat, a cargo five times its size. The ant moves like an old woman carrying her house after foreclosure, stubborn, regal, refusing surrender. I offer the ant a crumb of empathy, but empathy is not edible; it is only a larger burden. The ant disappears between the slats, marching toward a kingdom I cannot pronounce.

I once tried to mail a letter to the future. Addressed it: To whoever still feels like a glass of water on a nightstand when the world is thirsty. I stuffed it into a bottle, sealed it with wax from a broken crayon the color of sunrise, and tossed it into the river. The river sent it back three days later, wet and swollen, ink blurred into Rorschach bruises. I hung the pages on a clothesline, let solar winds read them aloud. The next morning the words had dried into something new: “Forgive the interruption, but your translation of longing is ready for pickup.” I have no idea who signed it—perhaps the chair, perhaps the ant, perhaps the moon practicing cursive.

Time is a subscription service I keep forgetting to cancel. It auto-renews at the worst moments—during dental drills, during phone calls that end with “take care,” during the precise second I realize I’ve been humming the lullaby my mother stopped singing. I keep meaning to downgrade to a cheaper plan, maybe the Bronze Age, when anxiety was called “omens” and could be solved by sacrificing goats rather than by scrolling. But customer service puts me on hold, and the hold music is always my childhood pulse played at half speed. I dance to it anyway, there on the courtyard bricks, shadow boxing with a future that keeps sending spam: Congratulations! You have been pre-approved for tomorrow!

Words left: 312. Enough to confess that I talk to dead people like they’re neighbors borrowing Wi-Fi. Enough to admit that I still flinch when airplanes leave contrails, half expecting a decimal point to slide loose and crater the city. Enough to tell you the chair is beginning to lean like a question mark, tired of supporting bodies that keep leaking memories. Yet it stays, loyal as an altar. I pat its back; it answers with a hairline crack, polite applause from the cheap seats.

I read once that the universe is flat like a sheet of paper, and we’re doodles trying to draw ourselves bigger than the margins. Tonight I believe it: every rooftop is a comma pausing the sentence of the sky. Every window is an eraser. The chair and I are doodles in solidarity, ink that learned to hold hands. If gravity fails, at least we’ll blur together, a smudge shaped somewhat like forgiveness.

Words left: 147. I thought I would use them to beg the years to slow, but instead I’ll offer them to you, stranger holding this page. Fold them into a paper fan and cool your face. Wave them at whatever burns inside you. If anyone asks where you got the breeze, tell them it came from a plastic chair that moonlight mistook for a cathedral. Tell them you left payment in the form of a sunflower seed, now traveling somewhere underground, proof that heaviness can be both cargo and map. Tell them the chair still stands, and therefore so do we, a quiet line of ink refusing the eraser.

Words left: 0.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
